prompt: Cinematic documentary photograph of an elderly Japanese bonsai master's workshop. In the center, a mature bonsai tree sits on a worn wooden turntable, meticulously pruned — its branches are deliberately sparse, elegant, capturing only the essential structure of a full-sized tree. On the floor beneath the workbench, a pile of freshly clipped small branches and leaves accumulates, showing what was removed. Beside the bonsai on the bench sits a pair of sharp pruning shears and fine copper wire coils used for shaping. The bonsai is healthy, balanced, and beautiful despite having less foliage than it could support — its restrained form reveals the tree's true architecture. Through a window behind, a wild, overgrown, tangled garden tree is visible — lush but chaotic, its shape lost in unconstrained growth. Warm afternoon light filtering through a paper screen. No diagrams, no graphs, no text, no labels, no mathematical notation, no equations, no floating annotations, no non-physical elements, no neural network visualisations, no data flow arrows. Everything in the scene must be a physical object that could exist in the real world.
metaphor: A pruned bonsai constraining growth to essential structure, contrasted with an overgrown wild tree — regularization penalises complexity to preserve generalisation, just as pruning removes excess to reveal true form.
